---
title: "Paper Lab"
description: "A research-reading workflow section for decoding notation, theorems, dependencies, and reading order before diving into theory-heavy papers."
page-type: module-overview
status: published
update-status: current
level: research
topic: paper-reading
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/proofs/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/logic/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Understand the recommended first-pass order through the Paper Lab section."
  - "Know when to branch into notation, theorem, dependency, or trail-building workflows."
  - "Connect site topic pages to paper-reading as a reusable skill."
applications:
  - theory-paper-reading
  - machine-learning-theory
  - optimization
  - statistics
keywords:
  - paper reading
  - theorem reading
  - notation
  - dependencies
  - reading trails
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: quarterly
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
stable-sources:
  - "Stanford CS114 reading packet"
  - "Stanford CS167 technical paper-reading guide"
  - "MIT Mathematics for Computer Science"
current-sources:
  - "Stanford CS114 course page"
  - "NeurIPS Paper Checklist"
---

## Why This Section Matters

Paper Lab is where the site teaches research reading as a skill.

A good paper guide should help you:

- decode notation
- identify assumptions
- trace theorem dependencies
- separate core claims from supporting details
- connect proofs to experiments and applications

The point is not to build a giant paper list. The point is to make hard papers readable by giving you a repeatable workflow.

## Recommended First-Pass Order

Use this order:

1. [How to Read a Paper](how-to-read-a-paper.qmd)
2. [Notation Translation](notation-translation.qmd)
3. [Theorem Decoder](theorem-decoder.qmd)
4. [Dependency Maps](dependency-maps.qmd)
5. [Reading Trails](reading-trails.qmd)

That order is deliberate:

- start with the global workflow
- fix notation before you attempt dense theorem parsing
- decode theorem structure once the symbols are under control
- map dependencies before full proof reading
- build reading trails only after you know how to choose the next page or paper on purpose

## When To Open Which Page

- `How to Read a Paper`: use first, before spending real time on any paper
- `Notation Translation`: open when symbols are the blocker
- `Theorem Decoder`: open when a theorem statement is the blocker
- `Dependency Maps`: open when the paper has too many lemmas, citations, or appendices
- `Reading Trails`: open when you know the topic you want, but not the reading sequence

## Core Skills In This Section

- `How to Read a Paper`: staged reading before you sink time into notation and proof details
- `Notation Translation`: how to turn dense symbols into typed objects, roles, and plain-English statements
- `Theorem Decoder`: how to unpack objects, assumptions, claim shape, and theorem-level evidence
- `Dependency Maps`: how to choose a reading order and separate topic prerequisites from lemma and citation structure
- `Reading Trails`: how to turn one research goal into a short, readable sequence of pages and papers

## Sources and Further Reading

- [CS114/214: Selected Readings of CS Research](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs114/) - `First pass` - strong model for organizing a paper-reading workflow around staged exposure. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [How to Read a Paper](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs114/reading-keshav.pdf) - `First pass` - the clearest short reading workflow for deciding what deserves deeper attention. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [CS167 Technical Paper Reading Guide](https://cs.stanford.edu/~rishig/courses/ref/paper-reading-technical.pdf) - `Second pass` - especially useful for theorem-heavy and implementation-heavy technical papers. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT Mathematics for Computer Science Readings](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-042j-mathematics-for-computer-science-fall-2010/pages/readings/) - `Second pass` - useful when your reading bottleneck is logic, proof structure, or theorem style rather than the paper topic itself. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [NeurIPS Paper Checklist](https://neurips.cc/public/guides/PaperChecklist) - `Paper bridge` - current venue-side reminder that assumptions, proofs, and scope boundaries need to be read explicitly. Checked `2026-04-25`.
